does: This paper conducts a comprehensive meta-analysis of existing AI safety benchmarks across dozens of models to empirically investigate whether they actually measure safety progress or merely track general model capabilities. The authors correlate performance on numerous safety benchmarks with upstream general capabilities like reasoning and knowledge, as well as with training compute. Their core finding is that many widely-used safety benchmarks show high correlation with general capabilities and compute rather than measuring distinct safety improvements, revealing a phenomenon they term 'safetywashing' where improvements in model capability are repackaged and presented as safety advances without genuine risk mitigation.
connects: This work builds on prior concerns about benchmark reliability and validity in machine learning, extending these critiques specifically to the rapidly expanding AI safety domain. It responds to and synthesizes the scattered landscape of safety research from recent years—including work on toxicity detection, adversarial robustness, alignment techniques, and various safety-focused evaluations—while drawing on longstanding literature on capability-agnostic safety metrics and benchmarking pitfalls. The paper connects to earlier meta-scientific critiques of AI evaluation practices and joins ongoing discussions about what constitutes genuine safety progress versus superficial improvements.
enables: By establishing an empirical foundation for understanding which safety metrics are meaningful versus misleading, this work enables more principled design of future safety benchmarks that decouple from general capabilities. It points toward developing safety measures that capture distinct risks and failure modes rather than capabilities, potentially guiding resource allocation in safety research toward interventions with genuine risk-reduction potential. The framework also enables practitioners and policymakers to critically evaluate safety claims in published work and supports the development of standardized, validated safety evaluation protocols that could prevent continued safetywashing in the field.
